text.title: VUI + IoT — Command Patterns and Multi-Device Coordination
text.summary: Command taxonomy for smart home voice control, multi-device coordination flow design, partial failure handling, and best practice rules for coherent IoT voice UX.

list.item: Direct control: single device command — "Turn off the lights" → one device, immediate execution
Scene activation: named routine triggering multiple devices — "Goodnight" → lights off, thermostat set, doors locked
Status query: read device state — "Is the front door locked?" → returns current state, no action
Conditional: state-triggered automation — "Turn on the AC when I get home" → registers a geofence trigger
Scheduled: time-triggered action — "Turn off the lights at 11 PM" → registers a timer

rule.statement: Design scene names to be discoverable by voice: short, memorable, natural-language phrases that match how users talk about activities ("Movie time", "Goodnight", "I'm leaving") rather than technical labels ("Mode 3", "Preset B").
rule.rationale: Scene names that match natural speech patterns are recalled and used; technical labels are forgotten and users revert to device-by-device control, defeating the purpose of scenes.

rule.statement: After a multi-device command, confirm all executed actions in a single summary response — not a separate confirmation per device. If some actions failed, report the failures explicitly in the same summary.
rule.rationale: Per-device confirmations are verbose and slow; a single summary is faster and still provides the information needed to detect partial failures.

example.code:
User: "I'm leaving" System executes: lock doors, lights off, thermostat set to 68F, arm security
VUI response: "Home secured. Doors locked, lights off, thermostat set to 68. Have a good day."
Partial failure response: "Mostly done. Doors locked and lights off, but the thermostat didn't respond. Check the app."
example.note: Partial failure is reported without alarming language; the user gets actionable next step (check the app).

rule.statement: Never report a multi-device scene as fully complete when one or more devices failed silently. Always surface partial failures to the user.
rule.rationale: Silent partial failures leave the home in an unexpected state — an armed security system with an unlocked thermostat is a minor annoyance; a silent failure to lock doors is a safety issue.

rule.statement: Support "undo" for the most recent multi-device action by voice command ("Undo that" or "Never mind") within a 30-second window. After 30 seconds, provide a scene command that reverses the previous state.
rule.rationale: Users issue voice commands in ambient contexts without full attention; immediate undo significantly reduces the frustration of misrecognized commands that change multiple devices.

rule.statement: For conditional and scheduled commands, always read back the registered automation with specifics before confirming: "Turning on the AC at 3:30 PM when you arrive home. Say 'cancel' to remove it."
rule.rationale: Future-state commands are easy to misset; reading back the specifics catches time zone errors, wrong device references, and misrecognized conditions before they execute.

rule.statement: Group devices by room and by function — not by technical ID. Users say "the kitchen lights", not "device-group-14". Design the schema to match room names as they appear on floor plans or as users naturally refer to spaces.
rule.rationale: Device naming that does not match natural spatial language requires users to remember a separate naming convention, reducing adoption and satisfaction.
